comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is almost here, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my forty-first published book and my second solo translation. The Art of Memory, an ancient system of mnemonic practice popular among Renaissance occultists, has been an interest of mine since I first learned about it in my late twenties; Giordano Bruno, who was burnt at the stake as an unrepentant heretic in 1600, was one of the great masters of the art of memory; and I like to translate things out of Latin in much the same spirit that other people do crossword puzzles, so a translation of Bruno's On the Shadows of the Ideas was a natural for me. Azoth Press, the publisher, brought out a fine edition bound in black Nigerian goatskin -- no, I am not making this up -- along with an ordinary hardback edition, and when those sold out, promptly issued a trade paper edition which is still very much in print.

Bruno's work is the high water mark of the Art of Memory and this book shows it. It's an intricate, demanding text that assumes the reader already knows the standard art of memory.  Still, if you've read Frances Yates's The Art of Memory -- still the best introduction to the subject -- and put some time into practicing the version of the art she describes in detail, you can tackle Bruno's methods with good results. You can order a copy here in the United States or at your favorite online or full service bookstore elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no more comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.content: Is particle physics run by Pluto? If so, does that mean that people will stop believing in subatomic particles and the standard model of particle physics by 2036 when Pluto's influence dies out completely?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't think that particle physics as a whole is Plutonian -- it has a strong Uranian vibe to me -- but there's a strong Plutonian quality in a lot of the theories that have sprung up around it. So many of them show up with vast amounts of hype and grandiose promises, and then slowly trickle away in a welter of inconclusive results; that's the classic Pluto profile. I wouldn't be a bit surprised if general relativity goes the way of phlogiston theory, for example.

replies.title: Re: Replication crisis
replies.content: I have yet to see anyone compile such a list. I think a lot of scientists are too worried about what would happen if they took an honest look at it! What I've seen instead are nervous comments, in articles by researchers about the replicability crisis (which is worth looking up, btw), that some of the most important experiments in their fields are just as problematic when it comes to replication; I've also been told by people in universities that these days students in physics and some other sciences do computer simulations of important experiments instead of actually running the experiments, because the real experiments don't come out right. It would be a fascinating research project, suitable for someone with access to a good university library, to chase donw the details.

replies.title: Re: Replication crisis
replies.content:
I too would be interested to hear which experiments don't work.

I earned an undergraduate degree in geophysics 20 years ago from a university that, at the time, had quite strict standards. We had to replicate all the basic mechanical, chemical, and electromagnetic experiments using 19th century equipment, and they all worked. That's why mechanical and electrical engineering work to this day; Maxwell's Laws, Newton's, Carnot, etc. are all accurate within the limits of the physical stuff we use every day (combustion engines, power lines, cranes, even computer chips at the nanoscale.)

It's possible that students are doing these experiments as simulations now because the universities don't think it's worth spending the time doing them physically. All the basic labs and coursework re-deriving everything from scratch took up a good two years of my university education, leaving less time for advanced work and electives that make one "well-rounded".

As far as I know, most of the Replicability Crisis occurs in medicine, where there is a vested interest in getting the results that please the funder.

--Ms. Krieger

Somebody mentioned above the MOND vs general relativity + dark matter debate - the only reason why I would expect MOND to win out in the long run is because it explains the anomalies in the orbits of the extreme trans-Neptunian objects in the solar system without having to postulate a most-likely-nonexistent planet nine. The extreme trans-Neptunian objects should still be observable into the deindustrial future, if John Michael Greer's prediction on the Hermitix podcast of astrological charts stretching all the way beyond the edge of the solar system a few hundred thousand years from now holds.

But the galaxies and the galaxy clusters further out into the universe, which usually are usually used as evidence for either side in the MOND vs general relativity + dark matter debate, are going to be unobservable whenever the large space telescopes stop working.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Wolfram-Gorard paradigm seems like it indicates a reasonable way a unification could work, and there doesn't seem to be any showstopping problem with it yet besides that all they know how to use it to model so far is time-evolution of quantum combinations of scenarios of different possible spacelike slices with different possible arrangements of undifferentiated bits of mass-energy in them. If I remember correctly, Sean Carroll didn't seem that down on it on the podcast I was looking at.

The paradigm is based on investigating patterns of rule-governed changes to abstract tiny structures of connectivity, to see when different sequences of changes lead to the same result. Somehow, when the rules are chosen correctly, this results in ordinary general relativity popping out at one "spatial" level, and in the relative-state interpretation of quantum mechanics popping out at a higher, "branchial" level; and the point of contact between quantum mechanics and general relativity, which is the connection between the quantum Hamiltonian and the mass-energy density, has an allegedly natural interpretation in terms of shared curvature-like properties of the connectivity structures on different levels. Wolfram also hypothesizes that we may be able to find a third "rulial" level of relativization, where there will prove to be an important physical interpretation of the idea of relativity of different interpretational perspectives.

The paradigm does require changing to a many-worlds perspective, and to a perspective where the possibility of describing a quantum system in terms of complex-number phases and amplitudes is sort of an unexpected accident -- in the Wolfram-Gorard system, the concept of wavevector phase doesn't appear naturally; the role of the quantum action is played by accumulation of divergence in parallel transport along similar sequences of possible spacelike slices in curved "branchial space", the analogue of Hilbert space.

I vaguely suspect that Wolfram's postulated third level of relativity might relate closely to the key of an insight for finding the point of contact between ordinary physics and the structure-forming tendencies that Dion Fortune was calling "tracks in space".

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I would expect the standard model of particle physics to be run by Pluto at least, because most of the experiments, not just the large colliders at CERN, are done by smashing protons or electrons at each other to release enormous amounts of energy to generate those particles, in the same way that nuclear power is generated by smashing atoms at each other to release enormous amounts of energy. When the Plutonian era ends, it won't just be the LHC that gets shut down, but all of these other particle experiments currently be funded by the American government through Department of Defense grants will also be shut down.

Also, particle physics / high energy physics as a whole is a dying field. Misha Shifman said so in an article from 2020 titled "Musings on the Current Status of HEP" which can be found here:

https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.00101

Due to the failures of the LHC to detect anything beyond the Higgs, many particle physicists are leaving or have already left the field for other branches of physics or for industry, and there are fewer and fewer new scientists joining particle physics, preferring other fields like astrophysics or condensed matter physics where they can actually make progress. There will likely come a time in the future where people won't care about the standard model of particle physics anymore, because nobody will be joining the field if particle physics isn't making visible progress in this Faustian society, and the institutional knowledge of the standard model cannot be passed down to future generations and will eventually be lost forever.

replies.title: (no subject)

The standard model predicts that neutrinos do not oscillate, but scientists discovered in the 1990s that neutrinos do oscillate. Now most scientists might believe that neutrinos have mass, but neutrinos do not have mass in the standard model, and there is no universally accepted replacement model of particle physics where the neutrinos do have mass, mostly because there is no evidence for or against any of the available replacement theories (sterile neutrinos, Majorana neutrinos, etc...).

comments.title: Applying the Planes to an Understanding of the Gods and Higher Modes of Perception than Archetypes
comments.content:
Good Evening,

With the waning moon, may things you'd like to see less of in your life fade away.

To Share: After writing up my current understanding of the planes, I decided to apply that to my current understanding of the Gods, and how I think an occult understanding of the planes helps solve some tangles that theology can get into (but not all of them, obviously): https://jpowellrussell.com/#what_are_the_gods_and_how_do_we_come_to_know_them

To Ask: Somewhat deep in a thread last week (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/292934.html?thread=50521926#cmt50521926), you talked a bit about Jungian archetypes and how they filter our experience of everything including of the divine, as they're pretty fundamental to how these incarnated forms make sense of the world. At the end, though, you said: "Only at a very high level of development do we begin to outgrow them[the archetypes], and then it's because we have richer forms through which to begin to apprehend the Divine."

Would you mind elaborating a bit on this? Did you mean higher levels of development in material incarnation, or above it? If the former, what do some of those richer forms look like? I understand if going deep into this would take a post or book, just curious to get a sense of the direction.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Applying the Planes to an Understanding of the Gods and Higher Modes of Perception than Archetyp
replies.content: The higher modes of perception come into play outside material incarnation, though you sometimes see mystics and sages who get a glimpse of them in advance -- that's why they have to use paradox and seeming nonsense to communicate their visions. Lao Tsu's writings are a good and easily accessible example.

replies.title: Re: Applying the Planes to an Understanding of the Gods and Higher Modes of Perception than Archetyp
replies.content: Thank you for sharing these thoughts. I have also emerged from the monotheist bubble but I am not as far on as yourself in putting my new understanding of divinity into practice. One thing I have found helpful is some recent reading about some Buddhist and Jain understanding of the nature of the gods. In these cosmologies they do not deny the existence of gods on higher planes than ourselves or that it might be possible for humans to enter into some kind of relationship with them. But these gods have definite limits to their powers and are also ultimately subject to impermanence, death and rebirth. I think I can wrap my head around that way of looking at things.

replies.title: Re: Applying the Planes to an Understanding of the Gods and Higher Modes of Perception than Archetyp
replies.content:
Thanks for your kind words, and you're welcome! That's certainly an interesting take, and it wouldn't surprise me if there's a lot of truth to it, but I've only done the shallowest of reading on Buddhism, and absolutely none on Jainism, so I can't say that it's affected my view much (so far). That being said, I'm glad to hear that it has been helpful to you.

Cheers,
Jeff

Another good example of experiencing the divine on our material plane is Aboriginal art. I've observed pieces, seemingly just colourful dots and lines on canvas, that lived and breathed - as if the Spirit had been given form by the artist.

replies.title: Re: A Pledge to Say the Pledge
replies.content:
To be honest, I didn't start the relationship. Mithras sort of initiated (no pun intended!) the start. So I don’t know how well my advice would help someone just starting out but not already feeling his presence and his call.

In the near-beginning of the relationship, I had barely begun offering him incense and some simple prayers when he twice saved my rear from auto collisions. The one and only time I’ve heard what one might call a “voice from heaven” was shortly after the very first save, the incident that would have been a likely fatal head on collision if not for a small but effective miracle in the last moment or so before a likely impact. And that “voice” (more a mental impression, really) was a clear announcement from Mithras that I was supposed to die that day, but he had intervened to spare my life. I am still not sure why, and much else that he has done for me since has been the result of offerings and worship.

Much of that worship is the basic routine one would engage in if you want a good relationship with a deity: incense, some prayers (I don’t really write them out beforehand; they’re extemporaneous prayers), and some discursive meditation/deep concentration on Mithras and his mysteries. I want to emphasize that last part, because I think it is key. As you mentioned, most of what we know about him and his cult (and that knowledge is sparse; the details and interpretations always very tentative) comes from archaeological research and interpretation of mostly iconography and a few surviving inscriptions. Combine that with some relatively vague references here and there in some Pagan and Christian sources from the first few centuries of the common era. A modern worshiper of Mithras really has to open themselves to the god and his guidance through mystical experience with him. Meditation is the best way to do that.

Another recommendation, and it comes from my personal practice, is to not ignore the earlier “versions” of Mithras: the Iranian/Zoroastrian Mithra and the Vedic Mitra. Based on my own “gnosis” of Mithras, I think he’s a deity who is pluriform. That might just be my Christian background (i.e., the Trinity) influencing my interpretation but he seems to be a deity that has manifested in several related but distinct forms or personages: Mitra in the Vedas, Mithra among the Persians, and Mithras to the Romans and their subject peoples. There are hymns, prayers, mantras, etc., that one can find from those traditions, and I’ve also used those with good effect. Check out the Rigveda and the Avestan Hymn to Mithras (Yasht 10) to get started.

I guess, if I were to reduce this to a few steps to get started:

1. Fire/light is an important symbol, so start off with an image of Mithras and a candle (real or electric). This is the start of a basic shrine or altar to him. You can, of course, pray to him anywhere but a dedicated space (he did, after all, have his own dedicated underground temples) shows him respect and gives him a "living space" in your home.

2. A simple offering, like incense or even water (the iconography indicates water had some importance), before this shrine. I like to offer him water and incense twice a day: once around sunrise and once around sunset.

3. Either pray from your heart or adapt a prayer from the Rigveda, Yasht 10, or something like Rudyard Kipling’s “A Song to Mithras”.

4. Meditate on the image of Mithras, or a picture of ancient Mithraic iconography, or one of the surviving ancient references to him and his cult. For example, Emperor Julian mentioned “the commandments of Mithras.” Those are now lost, but I’ve found it very useful to meditate on what they might have been. Reading “The Roman Cult of Mithras” by Manfred Clauss is very helpful to get the thinking started. Though an academic work, it represents the best introduction to modern Mithraic scholarship. I am sorry to say that older works like those of Franz Cumont are badly dated, and much of the conclusions are no longer supported by the latest scholarly research.

5. Archaeological studies indicate the communal meal could consist of pretty much anything. My main meal of the day is also my “communion meal with Mithras.” I place an image of Mithras on the table, just behind my plate. I then simply pray over the food (whatever I’m eating that day), asking Mithras to bless it, to bless me, and to fill the food and drink with the renewing power of his sacrifice of the bull. While I do not have a set kind of food for this communion meal, I have found that beef with whole wheat bread and grape juice seems to be especially apt at receiving and then transferring the renewing power of the bull sacrifice to my own being. I’ve had days when I was dead tired or ill by the time I ate my main meal, but after consuming the consecrated food (especially the beef, bread, and grape juice version) I was almost immediately renewed with energy and vitality.

6. And just let him guide you, discarding anything I've said if he indicates you should (up to and including everything I just wrote).

I hope this is at least somewhat helpful to you.

replies.title: Re: A Pledge to Say the Pledge
replies.content:
The phrase "Novus Ordo Seclorum" appears on the reverse of the Great Seal of the United States.

There is a very long history in the USA of occult interpretations of the Great Seal, and especially of its reverse with an unfinished pyramid topped by an all-seeing eye, which can be traced back as far as Charles A. L. Tottens' 2-volume work The Seal of History (1897). Early on this set of occult teachings came to include such ideas as that the English-speaking people were the only true descendants of ancient Israel (from the "lost" Ten Tribes of Israel), that all the Biblical prophecies were actually about the destiny of the English people all over the world, that Great Britain and the United States were Ephraim and Manasseh from the Tribe of Joseph, that the Prophet Jeremiah brought an Israelite princess, Tea Tephi, to Ireland, Joseph of Aramathea brought the child Jesus to Great Britain (to Glastonbury, IIRC) on a visit, and so forth. (All this is closely entangled with the notion that present-day Jews have no connection whatever with ancient Israel.) Totten's ideas have been picked up by a modest number of present-day occult teachers and their organizations, and there are quite a number of obscure pamphlets on the occult significance of the Great Seal and what is sometimes called "Mystic Americanism" or "Anglo-Israelism." I know of one home-grown tradition of Witchcraft (non-Wiccan) where the founder's parents and grandparents were heavily influenced by such ideas.

That said, it remains quite possible that Charles Thomson, who designed the Great Seal, had some esoteric teachings in mind as he shaped its reverse. There was more to that man than our usual history books tell. Not only did he serve as Secretary to the Continental Congress, but he also made and published the first English translation of the ancient Greek version (the Septuagint) of the Hebrew Bible.

Thomson also wrote a very cryptic letter on the design of the Great Seal to the then President -- IIRC, it was John Adams -- forwarding a memorandum of what he had had in view as he designed the Seal, and asking the President to judge whether it was worth preserving in the Archives of the US or not; if not, he asked the President to destroy the memorandum he had sent. The letter remains in the US Archives; the attached memorandum does not.

replies.title: Re: A Pledge to Say the Pledge
replies.content:
The popular culture has also taken that name in some other directions. Here's one that I think notable.

In the weird high-concept tabletop RPG "Mage: the Ascension", there was a cold war for the consciousness of humanity. One side was a coalition of magickal Traditions, each of which represented human relations to a different set of aspects of the natural and premodern world, like animal physicality and the cycles of nature, tinkering and experimentation, law and writing, and religion and mysticism. The other side was the "Technocratic Union", loosely like the Radiance in the Weird of Hali novels but focusing their magick through the obligate use of a complex apparatus of superscience theory and material culture. Different Conventions of the Technocracy represented the transformative effects of technology and institutions on natural and human processes -- finance and advertising, medicine and biotechnology, exploration and spacecraft, and manufacturing and automation, and then the ideological backbone of the Technocratic Union was the Convention called the "New World Order", representing psychology and propaganda.

(In the backstory, the New World Order couldn't agree with the finance-and-advertising Convention about how to steer politics going into the twentieth century, so they set up communism in some places, and the other Convention, the Syndicate, set up modern capitalism in other places.)

One interesting thing to note is that nowhere in the Pledge of Allegiance are you actually pledging allegiance to the United States. You're pledging allegiance to the flag of the United States, which you then explicitly declare to be a symbol ("for which it stands"), and what the symbol stands for is a republic of one nation, under the protection and/or oversight of at least one god, indivisible, with liberty and justice for all.

It's a loyalty oath, to be sure, but it strikes me as a loyalty oath to the American ideal - what America is supposed to be - more so than to America itself as it actually is.

comments.title: Question about Oaths
comments.content:
Hi John,

Grateful for this space as always.

I was curious about a certain detail in some forms of initiation. For example, in Celtic GD and Heathen GD (both of which I’ve read out of curiosity but neither are actively my path), there are oaths sworn. These include swearing not to engage in quarrels or squabbles with initiates of any Mystery School.

I’m uncomfortable with this but I’d like to understand how I might be missing something. The reason being I’ve met and worked with other initiates who turned out to be abusive and unethical, and I’ve chosen at times to call out such behavior in the interest of preventing said people from abusing others in the future, which inevitably has led to a quarrel (these people don’t tend to take to being exposed very passively).

I feel like I cannot swear an oath like this, knowing my conscience tells me there is a time and a place where engaging in a quarrel is the correct and ethical choice to make, as opposed to letting someone get away with whatever they want, whether it’s sexually abusing initiates or lying to initiates to play them against each other in some Machiavellian power play, or any number of other things I’ve either seen personally or heard about from others.

I understand swearing to strive towards maintaining good relationships with fellow travelers on the path, but to swear never to engage in quarrels under any circumstances I can’t abide by (with my current understanding). This is an issue I’ve known about and struggled with for years and am finally breaking down and asking you about it. Luckily for me, I haven’t been in a position to be asked to swear such an oath so far, but I have a feeling that the time I will be asked to is approaching. Any advice or insight you might have would be invaluable to me.

Thank you in advance.

replies.title: Re: Question about Oaths
replies.content:
If you can't accept such a restriction, it will be necessary for you to avoid receiving those initiations. If you take up certain initiatory paths, one of the things you need to accept is that it's not your job to police the occult community; your work is to build yourself up, not to tear others down. It's not as though there will ever be a shortage of people looking for reasons to denounce their rivals, after all!

Keep in mind in this regard that what you contemplate, you imitate. Focusing on other people's wrongdoing is not exempt from this principle.

replies.title: Re: Question about Oaths
replies.content:
In other words, if I see something happening that isn’t right (in a group, lodge, etc), I ignore it and assume karma will take care of the problem and focus only on my self-transformative work?

I do understand that focusing on things like this will harm the work I’m there to do and I’ve had experiences where it’s not mattered that I’ve said something to someone, and they’ve gone and been abused anyway, and told me about it after the fact. So it does seem like some people need to find out the hard way (some, or maybe all?)

You can see I’m still struggling, but what you said made this closer to making sense than it’s ever been for me. Perhaps if I meditate more on this, with your thoughts in mind, I can get over this problem and be ok with it moving forward.

Either way thank you for this.

replies.title: Re: Question about Oaths
replies.content:
That's correct. If you see something wrong happening in a lodge or a group that you're part of, it's quite reasonable to leave. If you see something wrong happening in a lodge or a group that you're not part of, obviously you shouldn't join it. If someone asks you your honest opinion of such a group or order, you can certainly give it, but don't start a quarrel or intrude your opinion when it isn't asked for. You have other things to do with your time and energy.

It's always a temptation for those with a quarrelsome nature to dress that up in the garments of moral self-righteousness. Confucius had good advice: "When I see what is right, I applaud it. When I see what is not right, I examine my own behavior."

replies.title: Re: Question about Oaths
replies.content:
Very good. The Confucius quote is certainly potent meditation fodder.

From this last, I guess I felt like the oaths were a gag order on giving an honest opinion as well, now that I think harder about it, and I’ve noticed you are willing to disagree with initiates of other Mystery Schools, so on some level I think I’ve been confused and bristling at my initial understanding that this was a blanket condemnation of saying anything negative about anyone.

Of course, I’ve seen myself dress up a quarrelsome nature in self-righteous drag. I’m not so deluded as to not have caught that at times.

The Confucius quote reminds of me the Jung notion that the things you find wrong in others are your own shadow projections. Always a helpful notion (when I remember it!)

Thanks for your input here, John. It’s very helpful and I was right to assume you would be able to help me look at this from a different (and useful) angle.

replies.title: Re: Question about Oaths
replies.content:
So noted. It's important to read initiatory obligations as though you're autistic -- something I have an easier time with than most ;-) -- and take the literal meaning of the words as they stand, without trying to intuit the kind of broader meaning that words in ordinary conversation apparently have to neurotypical people. Here's the specific clause from the Ovate grade initiation in The Celtic Golden Dawn:

"I further promise and bind myself to maintain a friendly and benevolent relation with all other students of the Druidical mysteries, refusing under any circumstances to take part in hurtful gossip or quarrels of any kind among the initiates of this or any other Mystery School."

That involves certain very specific requirements. You're setting aside the temptation to pursue personal grudges within the Druid community, denying yourself the dubious pleasures of hurtful gossip, and agreeing not to engage in quarrels among initiates of mystery schools. That's what it requires, but that's all it requires. It's a very good idea, in fact, when you receive an initiation with an obligation, to meditate on the obligation word by word and make sure you understand exactly what you have agreed to do -- and just as importantly, what is not included.

Who from your life (most likely one of your primary care givers early on) manifested similar explosions of rage, tried to suppress their bouts of rage, modeled rage for you, taught you how to rage, rewarded you when you raged, poured reinforcing energy into punishing you when you raged, or otherwise encouraged you to take on their unwanted habit? Often, all that's needed is to properly identify who it was that affixed some imbalanced energy pattern within you for it to then begin rapidly dissolving away on its own.

That can still prove quite a daunting challenge, as the original projector may have built up formidable defenses of denial and confusion around any personal incapacities they transferred onto you. If you can't remember anyone primary in your life who was obviously raging, consider whether anyone was fixedly "not raging" all the time. The brittlely, inflexibly happy and satisfied often turn out to be corked volcanoes, suppressing (or possibly projecting and transferring) their copious rage.

While correctly identifying your habit's original author and owner may initially feel like an amplifying of your unwanted habit, that's actually your clearing away of its occluding defenses in order to come to grips with what got inflicted on you and how foreign it may well be to your true nature. Naturally, there will still remain plenty of hard work to be done, complete with belated growing pains and reclaiming of discarded parts of your Self. Figuring out that you're carrying around someone else's heavy baggage on top of your own is the fastest, most efficient way I know of to radically lighten your load, so that you can get back to the fun of working on and with your own tangled energy patterns.

comments.title: An astrological speculation
comments.content:
Your work on the waxing and waning astrological influence of Pluto, and the coming-on-the-scene of Uranus and Neptune, has gotten me thinking of the implications within this framework of finding another planet in the outer solar system.

For those who have not heard of this, by analogy to the way that Neptune was discovered by observing gravitational effects on Uranus, there is some evidence that there is SOMETHING pushing around the various kuiper belt objects at the distant edge. UNLIKE the evidence for Neptune (and the spurious evidence for Pluto), this does not tell you where specifically the planet would be, but instead just gives you a map of its prospective orbit. It maps out as a only slightly tilted to the ecliptic, more than 10x as far as Neptune, with an orbital period of tens of thousands of years, and thus extraordinarily dim. It would require years of dedicated surveys with existing telescopes, or months of dedicated surveys with ones coming online soon, to find it if it exists. Regardless of if these ideas pan out, I'm fascinated by what the implications would be IF they do given the model of planetary influences and their connection to discoveries. This hypothetical planet 9 would be unique in that it:

- Would be expected to be between the mass of Earth and Neptune, dominating its region in the way Pluto distinctly doesn't.

- Would only be observable at all with the very largest instruments that exist today, at the peak of energy consumption of an enormous global industrial civilization, and likely be completely unobservable for smaller civilizations.

- Would have a period so long that it would spend longer than an astrological age in any given sign.

This feels like a very different situation to other planets. It would seem to me that with the Pluto model as guide, from the perspective of individual humans its discovery would be the rise of a nearly unchanging influence that would last for thousands of years, that influence changing only on truly civilizational timescales. I, for one, cannot help but think of your previous work on the potential rise of 'technic' civilizations and the end of the shortlived pulse of energy our civilization is processing now.

In the event that this work pans out, do you think that this influence would be important and long-lived?

replies.title: Re: An astrological speculation
replies.content:
If it turns out that there is such a planet, it would be interesting. My understanding, though, is that careful measurements of the masses of Uranus and Neptune once planetary probes went past them -- once you know how a satellite orbits a planet, you can calculate its mass to a fare-thee-well -- erased the apparent anomaly in Neptune's orbit that led to Pluto's discovery in the first place. (Typically for Pluto, it was discovered due to an overinflated claim of influence.)

My own guess is that there are quite a few planetary-sized masses of ice out there in the Kuiper Belt, and more in the Van Oort Cloud, but they won't come to our notice until and unless there's need for another planetary energy in human life.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Hi JMG. Do you know of any historical examples of occultists who took the maxim "to be silent" to the extreme, and put on a facade of being materialists to hide their involvement in occultism?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Every occultist did that during the centuries when being known as an occultist could get you burnt at the stake. It was still quite common in my youth, when your chances of keeping a middle class job would drop like a rock if your occult interests were known. One of my teachers, until he retired, was very careful not to be known too publicly for his very serious occult interests. Of course those aren't the people who ended up splashed all over the historical record...

replies.title: (occult) secrecy
replies.content:
"Keeping silent" is am old practice, not only for occultists. It overlaps with another practice, which used to be more widespread in academic circles: learning how to say things in one's own words, in ones own way, without using pre-fabricated terminology, or ideas, and without necessarily going into unnecessary detail about the larger context. Or using ordinary or proverbial turns of speech. (eg, "Only dried or drying twigs can be gathered into a bundle.")

In this way. one can say what needs to be said about some particular situation, without calling attention to the background ideas. Those who are already sensitized and aware will recognize that there is a larger context, and know what it is. Those who are not yet aware will feel that there's something there, but not know quite what it is, and yet find it attractive -- or repulsive.

The ability to recognize the thought behind a statement, even when the statement puts the thought in unconventional form, is a skill that used to be developed by occultists -- or readers of literature, for that matter. (That's very different from making things up!)

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: (occult) secrecy
replies.content: A very good point. We really do have a lot of lost ground to make up. (To use a proverbial turn of speech...)

comments.title: Experiences of American vs European forests, cont'd
comments.content:
Good morning, JMG. Hope you're well, and thank you for providing this space as always. Last week someone asked about the "feel" of walking in the woods on different continents, and I wanted to chime in here since I only saw that comment after the cutoff. Especially since the replies were mostly Americans, while I spend a fair bit of time in the Norwegian forest but don't have too much experience with the American ones (although I did visit the Olypmic Peninsula and Hoh Rainforest many years ago).

Anyway, I think the commenters last week were right that the European woods have a more "welcoming" feel to them. At least that's been my experience here, with the caveat that I usually don't go on overnight trips into the very deep and remote woods, so maybe that's just because the woods I'm most familiar with are closer to human habitation and "used" to being tended by humans? Still, my experience has mostly been that these places feel bright, open and welcoming, a landscape that wants to be wandered and appreciated. Especially where the conifers have been allowed to grow more naturally, since monoculture spruce plantations to tend to be more dark and desolate.

The part of the country where I live in the inland Southeast has a fairly welcoming, gentle landscape in general, with low rolling hills, fields and woodlands. It reminds me of the Shire, which is one reason I wanted to move here from the bigger coastal town where I grew up. :) Still, I've also spend time in more rugged parts of the country, where my family has had a cabin in the upland forest for many decades, and that area doesn't feel unwelcoming either.

On the other hand, an hour or two north and east of where I live brings the start of another bioregion with a different feel. It still looks similar, with mostly pine taiga, but it's "bigger", more rugged and remote. Whenever I've been there I've felt that sense of "indifference" from the land some of the American commenters talked about. Not unwelcoming as such, but more a sort of strictness and uncaring about human beings. And indeed, that region always makes me think of a miniature Alaska. It should be noted that the few surviving wolves also live there, while all the bigger predators have been exterminated centuries ago in the rest of the Southeast. Maybe that's a factor too?

Since this is MM, I figured I'd take the chance to ask a semi-related question at the end. In that same region to the north of me, there's a massive, majestic mountain that towers over the surrounding and mostly flat landscape. It's a striking landmark, and I'm sure it must have had spiritual significance going way back, even if I don't think there's much in surviving written sources. Starting in the 60s, though, that mountain was turned into the biggest ski resort in the country, complete with posh hotels and restaurants. I don't know how you feel about image links here, but here's a picture: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trysil#/media/File:Trysil,_Norges_st%C3%B8rste_skisted.jpg

So my question is: what would be the spiritual and karmic consequences of taking what's to my eyes an obviously sacred landmark and turning it into a playground for the rich and a site of crass consumerism and decadence? Maybe I'm just biased because I'm not a fan myself, but something tells me that can't have been a great idea, and that the mountain (or its spirits?) wouldn't appreciate what's been done to it in the name of profit.

replies.title: Re: Experiences of American vs European forests, cont'd
replies.content:
That I can't tell you. On this side of the water, the mountain wouldn't even notice. Robinson Jeffers' fine poem "Carmel Point" has a relevant theme:

"The extraordinary patience of things!
This beautiful place defaced with a crop of suburban houses—
How beautiful when we first beheld it,
Unbroken field of poppy and lupin walled with clean cliffs;
No intrusion but two or three horses pasturing,
Or a few milch cows rubbing their flanks on the outcrop rockheads—
Now the spoiler has come: does it care?
Not faintly. It has all time. It knows the people are a tide
That swells and in time will ebb, and all
Their works dissolve. Meanwhile the image of the pristine beauty
Lives in the very grain of the granite,
Safe as the endless ocean that climbs our cliff. —As for us:
We must uncenter our minds from ourselves;
We must unhumanize our views a little, and become confident
As the rock and ocean that we were made from."

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1) What do you recommend for avoiding ego inflation as it comes up during spiritual practice? I'm aware of some of the signs of it, which I watch for, and so far this awareness by itself has helped, but I'm curious in what other ways ego inflation might pop up and what to do about it.

2) I'm not sure if this is related, but what are the signs of heart center imbalances?

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) First, get used the fact that the ego is just your idea of who you are, your representation of yourself to yourself. It's not you. If you identify with it, you'll spend your life making a fool of yourself, because nobody in the world will ever see you the way you see yourself -- and that's just as true if you have a deflated ego as an inflated one by the way. (Is there anything so irritating as one of those people who are constantly going on about their flaws and failures, wallowing in their self-inflicted miseries?)

The way out of the trap is to recognize that you are not your ego. It's just a representation -- you could with equal truth say "just a brain fart." Pay attention to its antics and learn to chuckle at them. When you want to say or write "I'm a spiritually advanced individual!" or what have you, that's the ego playing silly games, and when you want to say or write "I am a miserable sinner!" or the like, that's the same thing. Generally speaking, whenever you begin a sentence or a thought "I am..." it's probably the ego speaking. Learn to attend to that, and to be amused by it, and you'll be fine.

2) That's really an alternative-health question, so I'll leave that to those more experienced in that field than I am.

comments.content:
First, I've gotten into the habit of being more respectful to all living things, seeing them as subjects rather than objects (which one of your blog posts delved into). So much so, I'll quietly beg the pardon of grass or hedges I have to cut. In fact recently, I smushed a mosquito that landed on my hand, and quietly said, "Sorry for killing you, but it was either that or you'd bite me. Nature set us against each other, so it has to be". Am I going overboard, or am I on the right track? I think it's the latter, but I'd like to get confirmation, as well as any other relevant info.

Secondly, I've read widely about world religion, although I overlooked Druidry in the past. Now that I'm catching up, I've noticed a similarity between Druidry and some Buddhist teachings -- notably the levels of reincarnation (hells, ghosts, animals, humans, asuras, devas) which are roughly analogous to where we can end up on the material, etheric, astral, mental, and higher planes; there are also similarities between meditative/tantric practices and magical workings. I wonder, how/why do these seemingly disparate traditions have so many points in common? Could there be a common source of ancient knowledge, or ancient intermingling (when Buddhism briefly traveled West during antiquity), or some other cause?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) If you're going overboard, so am I. I apologize to bugs on the rare occasions that I have to kill them, and talk to them when I catch them indoors and put them outside. They seem to appreciate it.

2) It's almost a matter of when Buddhism and Druidry haven't intermingled. Keep in mind, first of all, that we know essentially nothing about the teachings of the ancient Druids; the Druid Revival is a modern (since 1700, more or less) religious movement. It drew heavily in its first century or so from Greek and Roman Paganism and Pythagorean philosophy, however, all of which share ancient roots with Indian thought (the Greek god Ouranos is the Hindu god Varuna, for example) and also had massive connections after Alexander the Great's time. Later on, Druidry drew extensively on Buddhist thought when that came to the West, first by way of the Theosophical Society and then via Buddhist teachers. In the bubbling cauldron of Western alternative spirituality, Buddhism has long been a very popular ingredient!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You'd have to ask a Buddhist -- and it will probably depend on what kind of Buddhist you ask! From my perspective, the ego is not the self, it's the self's representation of itself -- basically, your idea of who you are, which by definition (human consciousness being what it is) is always so incomplete as to be false. To "annihilate the ego" is simply to let go of the representation and stop identifying with it, so you can see -- as Zen masters like to put it -- the face you had before your mother and father were born.

What our host said!

Nor does there need to be any deception for the placebo effect to work. I have sometimes used it on myself to good effect. The trick, for me, seems to be holding two seemingly contradictory things in mind as equally true: (1) this placebo is materially inert, and nonetheless (2) this placebo will indeed work.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is actually a tremendous book, one of my absolute favorites, and I'm fairly certain that Apuleius used it as a vehicle for transmitting secrets of the mysteries. For example, early in the book Apuleius is at his aunt's house admiring a huge piece of statuary depicting Acteon gazing at Diana's nudity, being turned into an animal and being rent apart by his own dogs. Later, his own profane curiosity about witchcraft gets HIM transformed into an animal, and, towards the end, he is going to be forced in to a "Tijuana Donkey show" where he is going to have sex with a debauched woman who is then going to have wild animals let loose on her and they will both be killed. This is a clear warning about being led into the esoteric by profane curiosity. Just about the exact middle section of the book is the story of Cupid and Psyche, which is another variant of the story of the fall of the soul into matter and its path to liberation. The story of the transformation into an ass and his liberation from that form is complete in ten chapters, ten being the sacred number of the Pythagoreans, and then his encounter with the Goddess Isis and his subsequent initiations occur in the Eleventh chapter, which, like initiation itself, stands outside the cycle profane time.
I hope you enjoy this book as much as I did. It's hilarious, and, at the same time, really, really deep.

comments.title: Universalist Druids and Temple Founding
comments.content:
Good Morning JMG and Commentariat,

I've been going back through the Way of the Golden Section and while pondering the squaring of the circle, I had a light bulb moment and went back to the section discussing how combinations of the three druidic elements combine to make the seven gates of the SOP. The SOP as presented essentially squares the circle elementally and thereby harmonizes celtic 3 element and continental 4 element worldviews. Then yesterday, I was discussing John Gilbert and the gnostic lessons, their seeming simplicity, and their surprising depth. It became clear for the first time that each lesson pairs a physical/bodily practice with a spiritual one. I saw it in a new way as solar and telluric, and in doing both being a form of slow-burn communion - an awakening of the Lunar current within.

I've gone back over the mini histories at the beginning of the lessons and orders you've passed down. I wonder if you could help me better understand the intersection and timeline of development.

1. The gnostic lessons appear to be clearly separate from revival druid work, yet much of the work suggests that it is deeply informed by the currents and unifying of them. Do you know whether they were inherited from the Universalist movement, or a creation/fusion from those who were also engaged in druid practices.

2. I realize that for a time there was an overlap of leaders of the Modern Order of Essenes, The Universal Gnostic Church, The Ancient Order of Druids, and The Order of Spiritual Alchemy. Do you know if these groups' practices were significantly modified during that period to harmonize them or if they were harmonious to begin with.

3. Do you have any recommendations for older sources of Universalist thought before the unitarian split?

4. I'm excited to see a FHR Temple opening up. I saw that membership requires monthly meetings. It may be early to ask, but is there a protocol for visiting? I'm thinking of myself (and likely many others here) who are engaged in those practices and have never been in a temple setting and aren't colocated to be able to meet monthly.

5. I note that the new temple is open to those working with the FHR, WotGS, MOE, and DMH. I know that there are people near to me who have advanced in one of those systems but I'm not sure that there is one system that two people share. In the Way of the Four Elements book, I note that to start a temple you need at least two people who have worked through Way of the Golden Section and WotFE. Is the ability to incorporate practitioners from those varied systems specific to the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose or could a Golden Section Fellowship Temple be opened with similarly broad membership, incorporating DMH and MOE practitioners?

6. Is the choice to call it an FHR temple indicative that, though members may personally practice any of the other systems, the temple ceremonies will be FHR based?

As always, thank you for this space and your counsel.

replies.title: Re: Universalist Druids and Temple Founding
replies.content:
1) The Gnostic Lessons were originally drawn up by Rhodonn Starrus but heavily modified by John Gilbert. I don't know of any earlier source for them in the Universalist movement.

2) They were reworked extensively at that time.

3) Here are 614 texts for you to start with:

https://archive.org/search?query=%28Universalism%29%20AND%20mediatype%3A%28texts%29%20AND%20date%3A%5B1800-01-01%20TO%201960-01-01%5D

4) We haven't settled that yet.

5) No, that breadth of membership is specific to the FHR. The Golden Section temple work is more specific, and only people who've completed the work of The Way of the Golden Section should participate in it. That said, there's enough overlap that you should be able to work your way through the FHR material promptly and start a temple that can accommodate people of many backgrounds.

6) It's more specific than that. The FHR temple will be using the FHR rituals as published.

comments.title: Gods that love gay men

I got behind on MM but several weeks back someone posted about what gods would be willing to work with a gay man.

I am a bi tomboy, so I sat down and meditated on whether the gods loved me, and whether my sexuality and gender mattered to them.

The gods I work with are the Welsh gods in the SOP (Celi, Ced, Hu, Sul, Esus, Elen, Mabon) and three Greek gods: Mars/Ares, Athena, Apollo. Once I have more space in my life I will likely take on the full Greek pantheon and have two.

When I meditated on whether the gods loved me, what came to mind immediately was there are different types of love. For me, my priority in this life is spiritual development aimed at Gwynfydd. From this perspective, they love me. The gods smile down on me as I work on myself. I felt affection when this thought came to mind. I think they would likely lend aid in this work if I asked for it. The couple of times I have, assistance indeed has come.

When I meditated on whether the gods cared about my gender and sexuality, I sat for several minutes with the kind of feeling like "You've reincarnated as a human for a while and have been all kinds of gender & sexuality; this doesn't matter." At the end I got the final thought "Did Apollo not love Hyacinthos?"

I hope this helps the commenter from several weeks ago. Anyway, good luck on your path and I hope you find something that works for you. Best wishes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There are any number of initiations that stand by themselves, or are part of a brief sequence of initiations that stands alone. 2) There are certainly mystical initiations, including the Buddhist ones just mentioned. Martinism is a Western system of mystical initiation, with three degrees.

3) Of course. You can always just do the work; that's a little slower, but not necessarily that much, and it spares you trouble with potentially dubious groups.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Writers since ancient times have usually had a very distinctive profile to their income. If you try to make a living through writing, you can count on being very poor at first, because it takes a while to find an audience and build a backlog of writings. I'll use myself as an example; when I first got into print, I would have been homeless in short order if my late wife hadn't had a job with a decent wage. Ten years later I was making as much as she was; twenty years later she'd quit working and we had more money than we'd ever had before.

The moral to this story: be prepared to live cheap and work hard for a good long while as you begin your writing career. If you can keep a day job and still write, that's your best bet -- unless you're lucky enough to have a partner or spouse who will support you for a while. No, the internet hasn't changed that at all.

2) Any of the standard methods will work. If I was in your place, I'd craft an affirmation and recite it a hundred times first thing each morning. I'd also put some time into visualizing myself writing easily and steadily, with words practically flying from the keyboard onto the screen.

comments.title: Sartre and being human and meditation: a personal anecdote
comments.content:
I was reading an essay by Jean Paul Sartre the other day and he has a part where he is talking about being. Following the steps of Husserl and Heidegger, he states that it makes no sense to talk about humans or the world if you don't take them together, given that all we know about humans and the world happens because we are conscious and we make the world happen within ourselves, so it is more important to examine being-in-the-world through consciousness, rather than the dogmatic materialism of the physicist that cobbles together facts. One cannot know like that. I don't recall exactly, but I liked a phrase he said that trying to know the human being or reality through the accumulation of facts is like trying to approach unity by appending more digits to the right of 1.976. That might create an anthropology, but it doesn't approach the essence, as the existentialists where concerned with.

Then I meditated and something strange started to happen when I was reflecting on how one really isn't born fully human, in the full sense of the word. One has gained access to be born as a human being, in a human body, to learn how to embody the divine patterns in him by totally becoming a living pentagram, a microcosm that reflects the above as perfectly as this solar system has managed. After all, it is like a potter's wheel and the human being gets molded out of those cosmic tides.

Then it went strange. At some point my focus point of awareness was outside my body as if I was very focused on something that I got lost in (not a projection just an awareness of space and being bigger than my physical body) and when I focused back into myself: I saw a planetary primate, with a mammal brain, breathing and thinking to itself, sitting on a piece of tree, enthusiastically and as disciplined as he can trying to become a better person on a spinning planet that moves through endless space-time and I wept. And it all felt so very human, animalistic and wild. I loved it. It felt as if I got introduced to my body in a way.

Questioning started and at some point, dealing with frustrations, I uttered mentally, unconsciously directed to Coel or Hu, or both I guess. "How hard can it be? A pentagram has just five elements after all". As soon as I finished saying that, for what felt like 1 minute under water but I am sure it was not even 1 second, I had the sensation of being fully in charge of the existence of my body and my mind, and it sucked incredibly. My thought process distanced itself way, waaay back and I found myself in a position where metaphorically speaking I was spinning 27 different plates and that was not even enough for my body to take a breath or send a proper signal. Then it stopped, I came back to my normal awareness and the thought ringing in my mind was "not so easy for 5 elements, huh? ;-)" and I cackled.

Thank you for making a push towards putting meditation in it's rightful place. When I am fed up, all I want is circle around, sit down and do my practices after a tough day, and when I am feeling a little ungrounded, all I want is work!

I didn't get to use it when it was in vogue so bare with me, but that is far out man! 😄

comments.title: Ancient Stoicism vs Broicism
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

In this video, Justin Sledge talks about the ancient Stoics' contributions on the foundations of Western occultism (as opposed to today's watered-down Broicism): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gx1av438mLY

Do you know of any specific magical system that is solely based on Stoic philosophy?

replies.title: Re: Ancient Stoicism vs Broicism
replies.content: I don't know of one nowadays; they existed back in ancient times, but most of the magical knowledge of the ancient world got tossed into bonfires once political Christianity seized power. You can certainly study the old Stoics, though, and see what connections you can make between their work and magical training. Epictetus is always a good place to start -- he's my go-to Stoic, not least because he's saved my sanity and my life in some very difficult moments.

replies.title: Re: Ancient Stoicism vs Broicism
replies.content:
Not JMG, of course, but in case any of this helps, I thought I'd share.

Not explicitly "Stoic," and not exactly magical systems, but if you wanted to look to religious material with an eye toward magical practice (topics to meditate on, Gods to call upon in banishing rituals, and so forth), you might find these helpful:

- Hellinismos is one of the attempts at reconstructing late classical pagan religion, a la the emperor Julian. I don't practice and haven't dug in, but this site seems rather thorough: https://www.hellenicgods.org/
- Also, Kaye Boesme has put together a lovely little guide to personal polytheistic practice that is meant to work for whatever tradition, but is fairly classical in its grounding, called The Souls Inner Statues, which is available for free online here, or for purchase through at least the big, slimy river, though used copies may have turned up elsewhere by now: https://kayeofswords.github.io/soulsinnerstatues/
- Some of the middle to late Platonists got up to some occult-ish stuff, like Iamblichus and his theurgy, and my understanding is that they mostly saw Stoicism as one branch of the common Hellenic/Hellenistic philosophical tree, rather than a separate school that had to jealously maintain its borders, so you might find some things of interest there
- Also, the whole Hermetic tradition was very classically inspired, so Stoicism is likely woven into much of it (or else it shares common threads with Stoicism, like a belief in transcendent virtue and an ordered universe)

Anyhow, I know none of that is exactly what you were looking for, but I hope it might give you some stuff to get going on. Worst case, an SOP invoking Greek Gods, meditations on Stoic writings, and a divination method of your choice (astrology is the only traditionally Greek one that comes to mind as one that folks still know how to do) could likely take you pretty far.

Whatever you end up doing, good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Ancient Stoicism vs Broicism
replies.content: Maybe it interests you that the spiritual exercises in The Wisdom of Hypatia start based on Epicureanism, go through Stoicism, and finish with Neoplatonism.

comments.title: The anti-gratitude curse
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
Today I finally found the time to ask you about a problem I've been having for a long time now.

My late mother was a person of strong will and little wisdom (note: not intelligence — she was very intelligent), which — probably predictably — led to a life of misery and heartbreak. Since she also firmly believed in a lot of high-flying ideals (basically the whole hippie philosophy of peace, love, and equality, but minus the drugs and the free love), the stark contrast of those ideals to the reality of the life she was creating for herself and her children made her develop a bit of spiritual paranoia, which crystallized in some convictions she unfortunately imprinted on us children, and which we struggle with to this day.

The worst of those was her belief that if you are too ‘loud’ in your appreciation of a good thing in your life, it would soon be taken away from you. Whenever some new catastrophe descended on us (usually caused by some shenanigans of our father), she’d remark, “Oh, looks like I laughed too much again!” The problem was that this belief evolved into some kind of self-fulfilling prophecy, because whenever one of us expresed relief or gratitude for something, we’d lose it in short order. As I said, she had a strong will; and I’ve long wondered if she didn’t, without meaning to, create a curse.

I certainly feel cursed. Gratitude is a fundamental attitude that is exhorted in every spiritual practice, religion, even pop-psychological self-help articles! Yet I (and my sister, too) don’t dare to be grateful, for fear that some sinister force could notice and basically go, “Oh my, how could we overlook this thing? Thank you for alerting us!” and snatch it away.

My atheist-materialist mind insists that this is totally irrational superstitious nonsense, but every time I try to prove to myself that it is, things go horribly wrong. The latest example is saying out loud how much I love my dogs and how grateful I am for having them in my life — a week later, one of them fell gravely ill and almost died. He’s still not cured of his illness, although he’s much better; I still don’t dare to express relief and gratitude that he ‘made it’...

So my question is: did our mother curse us? How do I break this curse, without breaking anything else in the process (like my dogs’ health)? I feel like I have a noose around my neck, drawing ever tighter as soon as I dare to move just a little bit!

(Due to time zones I won’t be able to respond to any answers from you or the commentariat, but I will do so on next MM! Thank you so much in advance!)

replies.title: Re: The anti-gratitude curse
replies.content:
Yes, that's a curse, or one of the kinds of curse. Trying to "prove that it's not true" won't help because you know that it's there, and the nocebo effect -- the placebo effect's nasty kid sister -- will guarantee trouble even if nothing else does. For the time being, accept that it's there, and avoid giving voice to gratitude.

Over the longer run, well, you have some work ahead of you. Have you by any chance done the Octagon Society work? If not, that might be a good way to start unpacking the emotional baggage of your childhood, which is what anchors curses of this kind. I would also recommend a daily practice of the Sphere of Protection, if you don't do that already, to start the process of clearing away unhealthy energies from your subtle body. It won't be a fast process but it will work.

comments.title: A strange synchronicity
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

As always, thanks for keeping this space open and public. It’s been a while since I last posted, but I’m glad to be back.

Something strange happened a few days ago, and by strange, I mean there was a synchronicity to it that makes me want to read more into the situation, so I’m here to get your insight if possible, or from any potential readers here.

I was getting ready to travel, leaving home for over 10 days, so I was emptying some of the perishables I hadn’t consumed and couldn’t really freeze due to a lack of space. At the same time, I was arranging the clean pots into their positions. Approximately two weeks later, I arrived home and immediately noticed a very foul smell upon entering the apartment. There were some dead green flies by the windows. I searched the kitchen and bathroom, but to no avail. I saw a strange little mass that looked like moldy rice; when I touched it, I realized it was actually many worms bundled into a strange ball. It turned out I had left a chicken breast in one of the cupboards by mistake, and a whole ecosystem, with its accompanying poignant smell, had basically come to life. I didn’t feel great about it; it felt eerie, and I’m usually quite comfortable with natural processes. I proceeded to clean using vinegar, lime, and later some of my frankincense to get rid of the smell, which was not easy. Getting rid of the maggots also took more than a day.

My mother, who lives in South America, would be visiting in a few days for the first time (I live in Europe), so I thought I might as well tell her what happened, as I didn’t want her to assume that smell was normal in my apartment. I must stress this has never happened before, and also, my mom is a big fan of cleanliness. Anyway, I proceeded to tell her over the phone, and she said, "Huh, funny coincidence... we had the exact same issue here at home (my sister’s place in South America), a piece of chicken, maggots, terrible smell... at exactly the same time it was happening in my apartment in Germany."

That’s when the same eerie feeling came back, because what are the odds that this happens at the same time in two different spaces of the same family, just before my mom is on her way to visit from place A to place B? I’m not sure what it means. I talked with someone I respect asking for a potential interpretatio. theyThis person mentioned thought there was an aspect of renewal after leaving behind the things that don’t work for us anymore, and how my mom’s trip to my home might have a deeper meaning that way, a resolution to an issue that has been held on for, for too long. This is the first time after my dad’s passing that my mother travels to visit me in Europe, its been 5 years of me leving and dad dying. Yet, I’m not sure about it, and I do wonder if there is something I should protect myself and my family from.

One interesting point: A few years ago, after doing my usual rituals at night and meditation in front of a candlelight (QC LBRP MP QC sequence)... after staring intently at the candlelight, I saw a green fly, just like the ones I found dead this time, slowly come to the fire and literally immolate itself in front of me. It was a strange occurrence to observe, and I actually asked here about it. If I recall, I was told it probably held some personal meaning. I can’t help but think that this is related as its the exact same green flies (end form of the maggots) that I found dead by the windows this time round (this fly/burning happened on my previous house tho, on a summer night). Just for good measure I threw 3 cards and had "the magician, reversed 5 of wands, and Wheel of fortune" which somehow points at me interpreting this through my fondness of a magical world view, of how its about resolving along standing issue that has created disharmony, and how its "meant to be"?

Anyway, thanks for the attention to this rather long post
Ps: I am the guy who was working on the ostrom environmental economics forest conservation PhD, if that rings a bell.

as always thanks for your help

Eol

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Hi JMG. Is there any occult dynamic behind the phenomenon known as "oneitis"? Like, creating an artificial elemental by obsessing over the idealized image of a specific person, and reinforcing it by continually lusting after that?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is one of the places where Carl Jung's version of occult teaching -- which is all that his psychology ever was, of course -- is extremely useful. We all have, hardwired into the deep places of our minds, a set of basic images that function as instinct triggers. There's a "Mom" image, a "Dad" image, an "enemy" image -- that's the Shadow -- a "teacher" image, and so on. One of the most powerful of these is the "lover" image. When it's triggered, it's the psychological equivalent of beer goggles; it projects, onto the person who triggers it, 600 million years or so of sexual instinct, which is why people get so giddy and stupid when it happens.

The sane way to deal with the "lover" image -- the anima or animus, in Jung's terms -- is to recognize that it's simply your mating instinct popping up, that it's no more rational or reasonable than an erection, and that you can choose to do something about it or not. Problems come in when somebody has that trigger go off and insists that, no, the person in question really does have all those qualities that are being projected onto her or him (or whatever), and starts getting obsessive. Yes, that can build an emotionally charged astral image, especially if it's further charged on the etheric plane through repeated masturbation.

Under most circumstances it'll fade out after two years or so -- one Mars cycle -- but it can be uncommonly dreary for everyone concerned before that happens. Common sense regarding your internal instinct images is more useful.

replies.title: Re: Druid Revival Gods and the Sphere of Protection

replies.title: Re: Druid Revival Gods and the Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
This may be getting farther afield than you're looking for, but building on our gracious host's mention of the Mabinogion, many of the figures within are often considered euhemerized Gods - like Rhiannon, Arawn, Lleu, Math, Gwydion, and others. The Four Branches of the Mabinogi by Will Parker has an excellent discussion of the (many) possible historical and mythical influences on these figures, and Taliesin's Map by J. Dolan tries to "map" figures from Welsh myth onto cognate Indo-European deities, and compares it to the Bhagavad Gita and Iliad, which other scholars have argued were also euhemerized/heroified versions of tales that were once about the Gods explicitly (haven't read this one yet, just flipped through it, but it has fascinating tables of correspondences between mythic figures/deities).

All of this may be veering too far into ancient reconstruction, rather than research into the Revival, and so as I said might not be what you're looking for at all, but thought I'd share in case it proves useful.

Cheers,
Jeff
